trajectory_summary: The call opens on a passive but willing customer, briefly touches genuine bill frustration, and then exits on a soft callback with no locked time, no bill captured, no co-decision-maker identified, and the commercial account complexity unresolved. Parker moved through the verify checklist at surface level and released Bernard the moment a scheduling obstacle appeared. The call's energy never built past the brief animated moment at 02:16 — it stagnated and then leaked at the close.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is unknown, but the call should be classified as a soft callback — not a booked appointment. Parker said 'I can call you tomorrow' at 05:14 with no specific time locked and no confirmation from Bernard. Per analysis_directives.soft_outcome_distinction, a callback without a locked time has materially higher no-show risk than a scheduled appointment. This is a fragile lead, not a sit.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Callback locking — converting soft exits to scheduled appointments
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Okay. I could call you another day.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Parker released a willing customer with no locked time, no verbal handshake, and no button-up structure. Per the schema's setter_compensation_model, Parker is paid on sits — a soft callback that doesn't convert doesn't pay. This is the most direct paycheck-protection gap on this call, and it's a repeatable failure mode: any time a customer signals time pressure, Parker's default is to release rather than anchor. One question ('morning or afternoon tomorrow?') is the entire fix.

key_moments.quote: Hey. This is Parker. I'm with Ion. I was just reaching out because we had a request here that you were looking at solar for the home. How you doing?
key_moments.stewart_read: Parker's intro contains none of the legitimacy anchors the schema rates as the primary scam-anxiety defense: no Tesla-certified framing, no form-fill reference, no state mention. 'A request here' is vague enough to read as a cold call. For a customer demographic that skews 45-70 and carries door-knocker anxiety, the first 30 seconds are the only window to answer 'is this a scam?' before the customer's guard goes up. Parker spent that window on nothing.

key_moments.quote: Is this something you're still looking at, sir?
key_moments.stewart_read: Bernard answers 'Oh, sure. Yeah.' and Parker moves on. The schema marks interest_in_solar as the highest-weighted verify item — depth_grading: true, 'THE EMOTIONAL ANCHOR. Depth matters more than the answer. Top setters spend 60+ seconds here.' Parker got a one-word confirmation and checked the box. There is no emotional anchor on this call — no bill pain story, no neighbor influence, no environmental motive. The entire pitch that follows has no emotional foundation.

key_moments.quote: Oh my god. I I'm paying I've got two two, gloves. One's, commercial, you know, because of that child. Anyway, it's two phase. So I have both.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the only moment of genuine emotional energy on the call. Bernard's 'Oh my god' opener signals real bill frustration — the exact emotional state the schema says should be flipped into alignment-against-the-utility. Parker's response is to estimate a combined total and move on ('Wow. That's a lot. Yeah. We'll definitely see what we can do to save you some money there.'). The bill is captured as data and dropped. No flip, no emotional alignment, no curiosity created. The schema's failure pattern — 'ask + acknowledge + move on' — is exactly what happened here.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. We have battery options that can go with solar.
key_moments.stewart_read: Bernard asks unprompted about storing electricity for the shop — a direct battery storage inquiry. Parker confirms Ion has battery options and asks if Bernard wants to look at that too. Bernard says yes. Parker then moves on without capturing what the storage need is (shop-specific? whole-property? backup vs. offset?), without naming the Tesla Powerwall as the specific product Ion carries, and without flagging this as a cross-sell signal for the closer. The enthusiasm signal is real — a customer who asks about battery storage before the pitch has even started is more qualified, not less. Parker acknowledged it but didn't anchor on it.
